allies.rationale_text: Premortem strengthens mental-simulation by turning imagined futures into concrete failure and contingency checks before action.
allies.source_quote: Best used when the simulation is tied to concrete thresholds, pre-mortems, or contingency plans rather than left as a purely imaginative exercise.

allies.rationale_text: Decision-trees help mental-simulation compare multiple branches and threshold-triggered outcomes instead of following only one imagined path.
allies.source_quote: multiple futures can be compared explicitly

allies.rationale_text: Systems-thinking supports mental-simulation by helping the team rehearse interdependencies, roles, and cause-and-effect before execution.

antagonists.rationale_text: Confirmation-bias corrupts mental-simulation when the team rehearses futures that protect an existing belief instead of stress-testing it.
antagonists.source_quote: mental-simulation conflicts with confirmation-bias when mental Simulation may reinforce Confirmation Bias by focusing on anticipated outcomes that align with existing beliefs.

antagonists.rationale_text: Optimism-bias-and-planning-fallacy distorts mental-simulation when imagined futures systematically overestimate upside and underweight friction.
antagonists.source_quote: mental-simulation conflicts with optimism-bias-and-planning-fallacy when mental Simulation can lead to Optimism Bias, as individuals may overestimate positive outcomes when mentally rehearsing scenarios.

antagonists.rationale_text: Commitment-bias interferes with mental-simulation when prior plans create pressure to reject simulated outcomes that challenge the current course.
antagonists.source_quote: mental-simulation conflicts with commitment-bias when prior commitments create cognitive pressure that causes the simulation to reject results that challenge existing plans.

structured_tensions.tension_text: Mental-simulation conflicts with confirmation-bias when rehearsed futures reinforce existing beliefs instead of pressure-testing them.
structured_tensions.source_quote: mental-simulation vs confirmation-bias: mental-simulation conflicts with confirmation-bias (confirmation bias) when mental Simulation may reinforce Confirmation Bias by focusing on anticipated outcomes that align with existing beliefs.

structured_tensions.tension_text: Mental-simulation conflicts with optimism-bias-and-planning-fallacy when rehearsed scenarios overestimate positive outcomes and underweight planning friction.
structured_tensions.source_quote: mental-simulation vs optimism-bias-and-planning-fallacy: mental-simulation conflicts with optimism-bias-and-planning-fallacy (optimism bias and planning fallacy) when mental Simulation can lead to Optimism Bias, as individuals may overestimate positive outcomes when mentally rehearsing scenarios.

structured_tensions.tension_text: Mental-simulation conflicts with commitment-bias when prior commitments make the team reject simulated outcomes that threaten the existing plan.
structured_tensions.source_quote: mental-simulation vs commitment-bias: mental-simulation conflicts with commitment-bias (commitment bias) when prior commitments create cognitive pressure that causes the simulation to reject results that challenge existing plans.

curation_notes.summary: The retained relation surface stays close to the source's strongest current-vocabulary interactions: premortem, branching comparison, systems rehearsal, and three explicit structured tensions.
donor_drops: Did not preserve broader latticework or digital-twin expansion because the current vocabulary already supports a tighter simulation-discipline surface.
open_questions: Signaling is source-real in some simulation examples, but the retained set favors the cleaner rehearsal-and-bias neighborhood.
